model_narrative: This is a routing problem. A salesman travels among a set of cities, and for every ordered pair of cities there is a known cost of going directly from one to the other. We decide which direct legs between cities to include in the route. The objective is to make the total cost of the chosen legs as small as possible.

sets.name: ii
sets.doc: all candidate cities on the map. Only the cities listed in i belong to this tour, and every leg of the route runs from one tour city to another tour city; the remaining candidate cities carry no legs

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the cities that are actually on this tour; the salesman must visit each of them. The first city listed is the start of the tour and its tour position is fixed to 1

sets.name: j
sets.doc: an alias of the tour cities i, used to index the destination end of a leg

params.name: c
params.doc: travel cost of going directly from the first city to the second city

vars.name: x
vars.doc: 1 if the route goes directly from the first city to the second, 0 otherwise. A city cannot follow itself so the same-city legs are fixed to 0

vars.name: u
vars.domain: Reals in [1, number of tour cities]
vars.doc: the position of a city in the visiting order along the tour; the start city is fixed at position 1

vars.name: z
vars.doc: total travel cost of the route

description: Define the route's total travel cost as the cost of every chosen leg added up over all pairs of tour cities, and set the objective value equal to that total.

description: Each tour city must be left exactly once. For every city, the salesman departs from it to exactly one other city.

description: The chosen legs must form one single tour through all the cities rather than several smaller disconnected loops. Use the tour-position variables to order the cities so that whenever a leg from one city to another is used, the destination city comes later in the order than the origin city, which rules out any sub-loop that does not pass through the start city.
expected_pyomo:
n = len(model.i)
first_city = list(model.i)[0]
def mtz_rule(model, i, j):
    if i == j or i == first_city or j == first_city:
        return Constraint.Skip
    return model.u[i] - model.u[j] + n * model.x[i, j] <= n - 1
model.mtz = Constraint(model.i, model.j, rule=mtz_rule)

model_narrative: This is a routing problem. A salesman travels among a set of cities, and for every ordered pair of cities there is a known cost of going directly from one to the other. We decide which direct legs between cities to include in the route. The objective is to make the total cost of the chosen legs as small as possible.

sets.name: ii
sets.doc: all candidate cities on the map. Only the cities listed in i belong to this tour, and every leg of the route runs from one tour city to another tour city; the remaining candidate cities carry no legs

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the cities that are actually on this tour; the salesman must visit each of them. The first city listed is the start of the tour and its tour position is fixed to 1

sets.name: j
sets.doc: an alias of the tour cities i, used to index the destination end of a leg

params.name: c
params.doc: travel cost of going directly from the first city to the second city

vars.name: x
vars.doc: 1 if the route goes directly from the first city to the second, 0 otherwise. A city cannot follow itself so the same-city legs are fixed to 0

vars.name: u
vars.domain: Reals in [1, number of tour cities]
vars.doc: the position of a city in the visiting order along the tour; the start city is fixed at position 1

vars.name: z
vars.doc: total travel cost of the route

description: To build the complete model, enforce the following relationships in order. First, define the route's total travel cost as the cost of every chosen leg added up over all pairs of tour cities, and set the objective value equal to that total. Second, each tour city must be left exactly once. For every city, the salesman departs from it to exactly one other city. Third, each tour city must be entered exactly once. For every city, the salesman arrives at it from exactly one other city. Finally, the chosen legs must form one single tour through all the cities rather than several smaller disconnected loops. Use the tour-position variables to order the cities so that whenever a leg from one city to another is used, the destination city comes later in the order than the origin city, which rules out any sub-loop that does not pass through the start city.
